assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the most important actions taken when creating a new nation is establishing trade with other countries. As a newly founded nation, that is exactly what the United States of America set out to do. The United States traded for things like silk, gold, and tea, while other nations traded for everything from sea otter and seal pelts to sandalwood, opium, cotton, and tortoiseshell. As the United States navigated new waters—both literally and figuratively—they would come into contact with a mighty trade nation that had a lot to offer them, including the ever-important tea. However, China had its own rules and regulations as well as its own system of justice that was very different from the United States.
As you read through the chapter, we invite you to ponder what it must have been like to navigate these new waters and to meet new peoples whose way of life was so different from yours. What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? You will reflect on these questions after the reading.

➜ ANNOTATE
When America First Met China is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.146-160 "The return of peace" - “exotic name 'bêche-de-mer.'”

➜ MAP WORK
Familiarize yourself with the map at the beginning of When America First Met China.

➜ WRITE
Answer the following questions in 3 paragraphs: What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? What things made you uncomfortable and why?

• STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the ways we connect with history is through primary sources. A primary source is any firsthand account of history. These sources can be a journal, a map, a song, a picture, and much more. A good historian will always rely heavily on primary sources when researching or writing history. Eric Jay Dolin has also relied heavily on these primary sources. As you go through the next section, pay attention to the documents Eric Jay Dolin is mentioning. You will reflect on these primary sources after the reading.
For more about a primary source and a secondary source, view the video provided in the Extra Helpings.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.161-176 “Bêche-De-Mer,” - "select promising opportunities."

➜ WRITE
Answer the following questions in two paragraphs: What are the primary sources Eric Jay Dolin is using to support his storytelling? Why might these be good or bad primary sources to rely on?

➜ PONDER
What are some other books you've read that have used primary sources? Were they reliable? What makes a primary source unreliable?

• EXTRA HELPING
∞ Video Link: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
In this chapter, you will encounter a woman named Harriet Low who has recounted many of her adventures in journals. Harriet Low talked much about her time in an area of China that was forbidden to women, and her journals are insightful to anyone interested in history. Her journals are a primary source.
Throughout the coming chapters, Eric Jay Dolin will rely heavily upon primary sources to help his storytelling, while keeping it factual and correct. As you read through the next section, pay attention to the documents Eric Jay Dolin is mentioning.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.176-192 "The American Community" - end of chapter

➜ WRITE
Answer the question in one paragraph: aside from Harriet Low's journals, what are some other primary sources Eric Jay Dolin is using to support his storytelling?

• EXTRA HELPING
∞ Video Link: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
In Chapter 7, Eric Jay Dolin will explore China through American eyes. Specifically, he will explore how the nation came to be perceived as one with rampant poverty, horrible treatment of women, odd food and drink, and a violent government system that killed innocents. But were any of these things rooted in truth? Or were they just the ramblings of one people group encountering another nation with foreign eyes? It really didn't matter in the end. These people's writings influenced the perception of the nation of China.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.193-208

➜ WRITE
In three paragraphs, take time to reflect on what influenced the perception of the Chinese people.

➜ PONDER
How is your nation perceived?

• STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS

• What did you learn from your reading?

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
A Historian named Ian Barrow wrote a short history of the East India Company, which operated at the end of the sixteenth century all the way through the middle of the nineteenth. In his book, he writes about the beginning of this company, saying, "Toward the end of the sixteenth century, a group of English merchants based in London realized that there was an opportunity to make money in the spice trade." The British East India Company would exist for over 200 years and end up becoming one of the largest monopolies on trade. Additionally, it was known for having its own armies. The army at times reached 260,000 soldiers strong.
The British government began to see the company for the threat it was and eliminated its monopoly in 1833. From then on, trade would look very different. The parliament would appoint people to government positions, and these men would be faced with many challenges as they sought to build ties with China amid a growing conflict.
If you want to learn more or review the British East India Company, check out the short video provided in the Extra Helpings.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.223-242 "After the British" - "stage of the war."

• EXTRA HELPING
∞ Video Link: The East India Company || 400 Years: Britain & India || Episode 1

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
As you finish reading this chapter on the Opium War, I invite you to think on a few questions as you read though the rest of this chapter. Who was in the right? Who was in the wrong? How did each nation navigate this war? How do you think this impacted the trade relationship?

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE

➜ WRITE
In one paragraph, express how you see the participation of each nation mentioned in the chapter in the Opium War. What surprised you about this chapter?

• STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
How long do you wait for your packages? Perhaps if you and your family have fast shipping, you'll only have to wait one or two days for your order to arrive at your doorstep. In the 1800s, it would take weeks for one ship to get to the other side of the world and pick up the items, and then weeks to return. So, as one can imagine, there was a need to be the fastest ship out there in order to get the items back home and sold as quickly as possible. Many people took on the challenge of building these ships.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9 p.265-277 "At nine in the" - "American invasion began."

➜ PONDER
Think about how shipping and trade have changed over the years.

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the horrible parts about reading through history is coming face-to-face with the reality of sinful humanity. John Fea writes, "Indeed, anyone who studies the past realizes that there are no heroes in history. While people may perform heroic acts, all humans are tainted by sin and are susceptible to acting in ways that preference themselves over others and God. Historians understand, perhaps better than most, the reality of the pain, suffering, injustice, anger, and vice brought on by sin. In other words, they understand the tragic dimensions of life."
Exploitation is just one of the many injustices taking place in the 1800s. As you read through the chapter, you are allowed to feel heartbroken, angry, and disappointed at the treatment of those who are also made in God's image. Both belief in Christ and in the depravity of humanity can help us better navigate history. As John Fea writes, "Similarly, a belief in depravity helps us to better explain the human condition—the restlessness, the search for meaning, and the prideful ambition that has defined much of the past, especially in the modern era. Augustine was quite correct when he opened his Confessions with the famous words, 'Our hearts are restless until they rest in you.'”

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9-10 p.277-290 "Ever since the" - "the coolie trade."

➜ PONDER
Think more on Augustine's famous words, "Our hearts are restless until they rest in you."

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ RECAP
Think back to where you left off.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.10 p.290-308 "The plantation economies" - end of chapter

• STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ CATCH UP
Catch up on any reading you've not completed.

➜ WRITE
If you have completed all the reading, I invite you to read the Epilogue and answer the following question in two paragraphs: How does reading the history of China's relationship with other nations inform our modern-day interactions?

Exam Week

Answer question(s) related to course.

When America First Met China

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Erebus

➜ INTRO
What happens when nations find themselves with war vessels, but no wars? For 1800s Britain, the answer was to send its ships on exploration missions. The Erebus was just one of the ships that made the transition from military vessel to a vessel used for research and exploration. The captains and crew of the vessel changed over the many years it spent on the seas.
In Erebus, you will read a book about a legendary ship. However, there is another benefit to Michael Palin’s book, which makes it a helpful read as well as an enjoyable one. Erebus does not just share the story of the ship itself, but of the world in which this ship sailed. As you read through Erebus, take note of the names being mentioned in each chapter.
I also invite you to adventure with the people on Erebus and on the HMS Terror. Pay attention to the letters and journals that are being mentioned and remember that these were real people who experienced a world very unlike our own. And yet, you’ll also see a world that is very familiar too.

➜ ANNOTATE
Erebus is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Prologue-Ch.1 p.1-16

➜ WRITE

⍞ Materials: Blood and Iron

➜ INTRO
Two significant things happened in 1815. First, was the battle of waterloo where Napoleon was finally defeated. The second was the birth of a man named Otto Von Bismarck. Katja Hoyer writes, “His childhood, just like that of most Germans growing up at the time, was heavily coloured by stories of the struggle against the French.” Otto Von Bismarck would become essential to the rise of the German Empire.
As you read through this account of Germany’s history, make note of names and dates that seem important to you. Also make note of any events that are either happening alongside the events in Germany.

➜ ANNOTATE
Erebus is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.
